explanation.whyCorrect: The left and right crura are the posterior attachments of the diaphragm to the lumbar vertebrae. They are tendinous structures that anchor the diaphragm to L1-L4, converging anterior to the aorta where they are joined by the median arcuate ligament.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The central tendon is the dense aponeurosis at the center of the diaphragm where the muscle fibers insert. It attaches to the pericardium above, not to the lumbar vertebrae.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The costal cartilages are the anterior connections of the ribs to the sternum. The diaphragm attaches to the lowest six ribs, but this describes the costal (lateral) origin, not the posterior lumbar attachment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pericardium attaches to the central tendon of the diaphragm superiorly. It is not the posterior vertebral attachment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The median arcuate ligament connects the two crura anterior to the aorta, but the actual attachment to the vertebrae is via the crura themselves.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: During a forceful inspiration in a patient with respiratory distress, several accessory muscles are recruited. Which of the following muscles is an accessory muscle of inspiration?

explanation.summary: The sternocleidomastoid is an accessory muscle of forced inspiration. Along with the scalene muscles and pectoralis muscles, it elevates the rib cage to expand lung volume during respiratory distress.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sternocleidomastoid muscle acts as an accessory inspiratory muscle by elevating the sternum and upper rib cage when the head is fixed, increasing thoracic volume during forced or labored breathing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rectus abdominis is an abdominal wall muscle that assists with forced expiration by compressing the abdominal contents upward against the diaphragm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Internal oblique is an abdominal wall muscle that assists with forced expiration, not inspiration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transversus abdominis is the deepest abdominal muscle. It assists with forced expiration by compressing the abdomen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Internal intercostals assist with expiration by depressing the ribs and decreasing thoracic volume.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: The diaphragm receives its primary blood supply from the inferior phrenic arteries. These arteries arise from which of the following vessels?

explanation.summary: The inferior phrenic arteries are the primary blood supply to the inferior surface of the diaphragm. They arise directly from the abdominal aorta, typically just below the diaphragm.
explanation.whyCorrect: The inferior phrenic arteries branch directly from the abdominal aorta (or occasionally from the celiac trunk) and supply the inferior surface of the diaphragm. They represent the major arterial supply to the muscle.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The internal thoracic artery gives off the pericardiacophrenic and musculophrenic arteries, which supply the superior diaphragm, but these are secondary sources. The primary supply is from the inferior phrenic arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The thoracic aorta gives off posterior intercostal arteries and some bronchial arteries. The inferior phrenic arteries arise below the diaphragm from the abdominal aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The celiac trunk is an occasional alternate origin for the inferior phrenic arteries, but the typical and most common origin is the abdominal aorta directly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The superior mesenteric artery supplies the midgut. It does not give off branches to the diaphragm.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: The order of structures in the intercostal neurovascular bundle from superior to inferior within the costal groove is commonly remembered using a mnemonic. Which of the following correctly lists these structures from superior to inferior?

explanation.summary: Within the costal groove along the inferior border of each rib, the neurovascular bundle runs in the order vein, artery, nerve (VAN) from superior to inferior.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mnemonic VAN describes the arrangement from superior to inferior: Vein (most superior, closest to the rib), Artery (middle), and Nerve (most inferior). This arrangement is important for procedures like thoracentesis to avoid damaging these structures.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the order. The nerve is the most inferior structure, not the most superior.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This places the artery most superiorly. The vein is the most superior structure in the costal groove.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This places the nerve between the vein and artery. The correct order is VAN (vein, artery, nerve).
whyWrongByChoice.E: This places the nerve first. The vein is the most superior structure, not the nerve.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: Ribs 11 and 12 are classified as floating ribs. What distinguishes floating ribs from true and false ribs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not articulate with the thoracic vertebrae posteriorly

choices.label: B
choices.text: They do not attach to the sternum or costal margin anteriorly

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are shorter than all other ribs

choices.label: E
choices.text: They do not have intercostal muscles between them

explanation.summary: Floating ribs (ribs 11 and 12) are so named because they do not attach to the sternum or to the costal margin anteriorly. They articulate with the thoracic vertebrae posteriorly but have free anterior ends.
explanation.whyCorrect: Floating ribs lack any anterior attachment to the sternum or the costal cartilage of other ribs. True ribs (1-7) attach directly to the sternum, and false ribs (8-10) attach indirectly via shared costal cartilage. Floating ribs have neither connection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: All 12 pairs of ribs, including floating ribs, articulate posteriorly with the thoracic vertebrae.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Floating ribs do have small costal cartilage tips at their anterior ends; they simply do not connect to the sternum or costal margin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While ribs 11 and 12 are relatively short, rib length varies across the rib cage. Being short is not the defining characteristic of floating ribs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intercostal muscles are present between all ribs. The T11 intercostal space has intercostal muscles between ribs 11 and 12.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: Sensory innervation from the central portion of the diaphragm travels to the spinal cord via the phrenic nerve. However, sensory innervation from the peripheral diaphragm is conveyed by which of the following?

explanation.summary: Sensory innervation of the diaphragm is divided: the central portion is innervated by the phrenic nerve, while the peripheral portion is supplied by intercostal and subcostal nerves from the lower thoracic segments.
explanation.whyCorrect: The peripheral diaphragm receives sensory innervation from the lower intercostal nerves (T5-T11) and subcostal nerve (T12). This is distinct from the central diaphragm, which is sensory via the phrenic nerve (C3-C5).

whyWrongByChoice.A: The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic innervation to thoracic and abdominal viscera. It does not provide sensory innervation to the diaphragm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The greater splanchnic nerve carries sympathetic fibers to the abdominal viscera. It does not innervate the diaphragm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While the phrenic nerve innervates the central diaphragm (both motor and sensory), the peripheral diaphragm sensory innervation comes from the intercostal and subcostal nerves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The thoracodorsal nerve innervates the latissimus dorsi muscle. It has no role in diaphragmatic innervation.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: During normal quiet breathing, the diaphragm typically moves approximately:

explanation.summary: During normal quiet breathing, the diaphragm excursion is approximately 3 to 5 cm. During forced breathing, the diaphragm can move more extensively, ascending as high as the T5 vertebral level during forced expiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diaphragm moves approximately 3-5 cm during normal tidal breathing. This relatively small excursion is sufficient for quiet ventilation. Greater excursion occurs during deep breathing or forced ventilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.5-1 cm would represent minimal movement, less than typical quiet breathing excursion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 8-10 cm would represent extreme diaphragmatic excursion, well beyond what occurs during quiet breathing. This might approach the range during maximal forced breathing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 12-15 cm exceeds the total height of the diaphragm dome. This degree of excursion is not physiologically possible.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 1-2 mm is far too small. The diaphragm moves centimeters, not millimeters, during normal breathing.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: The intercostal nerves that innervate the intercostal muscles originate from which spinal cord levels?

explanation.summary: The intercostal nerves originate from spinal cord levels T1-T11 and travel in the intercostal spaces alongside the intercostal arteries and veins at the lower edge of each rib.
explanation.whyCorrect: Each intercostal nerve arises from the corresponding thoracic spinal level (T1-T11) and innervates the intercostal muscles and overlying skin of that intercostal space. The subcostal nerve from T12 runs below the 12th rib.

whyWrongByChoice.A: C3-C5 gives rise to the phrenic nerve, which innervates the diaphragm, not the intercostal muscles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: C6-C8 contributes to the brachial plexus for upper extremity innervation, not the intercostal muscles.
whyWrongByChoice.D: T12 gives rise to the subcostal nerve (below the 12th rib), and L1-L2 contributes to the lumbar plexus. These do not form the intercostal nerves proper.
whyWrongByChoice.E: L1-L4 contributes to the lumbar plexus (iliohypogastric, ilioinguinal, femoral nerves). These do not innervate the intercostal muscles.

questions.topicName: Anatomy of the Diaphragm, Ribs, and Intercostal Muscles
questions.stem: A thoracentesis is being performed in the midaxillary line. To access the costodiaphragmatic recess for fluid drainage, the procedure is most commonly performed at which intercostal space?

explanation.summary: Thoracentesis is most commonly performed at the 7th-9th intercostal space in the midaxillary line to access the costodiaphragmatic recess where pleural fluid accumulates. This location avoids injury to the diaphragm and abdominal organs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The 7th-9th intercostal space in the midaxillary line overlies the costodiaphragmatic recess, the most dependent part of the pleural space where fluid collects. This location provides safe access above the diaphragm while avoiding the neurovascular bundle.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The 2nd-3rd intercostal space is too high to access the dependent costodiaphragmatic recess where fluid accumulates. This level is used for other procedures like chest tube placement for pneumothorax.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The 4th-5th intercostal space is the typical location for chest tube insertion for pneumothorax, not for accessing the costodiaphragmatic recess for fluid drainage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The 10th-11th intercostal space risks puncturing below the diaphragm and injuring abdominal organs such as the liver or spleen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The 1st intercostal space is at the apex of the chest. It is not used for fluid drainage from the costodiaphragmatic recess.
